# Jupiter

Jupiter is the fifth planet from the Sun and the largest in the Solar System —
a gas giant more than twice as massive as all other planets combined.

## Quick Facts

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Order from Sun | 5th |
| Diameter | 139,820 km |
| Mass | 1.90 × 10²⁷ kg (318 Earth) |
| Average distance from Sun | 778.5 million km (5.20 AU) |
| Orbital period | 11.86 Earth years |
| Rotation period | 9.93 hours |
| Moons | 95+ confirmed |
| Cloud-top temperature | ~−145 °C |

## Overview

Jupiter is composed mostly of hydrogen and helium, similar to the Sun. It has
no solid surface — its atmosphere gradually transitions into a liquid metallic
hydrogen interior. Jupiter rotates faster than any other planet, completing a
day in under 10 hours.

## Notable Features

- **The Great Red Spot** — a giant storm larger than Earth that has raged for
  at least 350 years.
- **The Galilean moons** — Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto, discovered by
  Galileo in 1610.
- **A faint ring system** and the strongest magnetic field of any planet.

## Exploration

- **Pioneer** and **Voyager** probes flew by in the 1970s.
- **Galileo** orbited from 1995 to 2003.
- **Juno** has been studying Jupiter's interior and poles since 2016.

# Saturn

Saturn is the sixth planet from the Sun and the second largest, famous for its
spectacular ring system.

## Quick Facts

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Order from Sun | 6th |
| Diameter | 116,460 km |
| Mass | 5.68 × 10²⁶ kg (95 Earth) |
| Average distance from Sun | 1.43 billion km (9.58 AU) |
| Orbital period | 29.4 Earth years |
| Rotation period | 10.7 hours |
| Moons | 146+ confirmed |
| Cloud-top temperature | ~−178 °C |

## Overview

Saturn is a gas giant composed mainly of hydrogen and helium. It is the least
dense planet in the Solar System — so light that it would float in water if a
large enough ocean existed. Its low density gives it a noticeably flattened,
oblate shape.

## Notable Features

- **The rings** — made of billions of ice and rock particles ranging from dust
  grains to house-sized chunks, spanning up to 282,000 km wide but only about
  10 m thick on average.
- **Titan** — Saturn's largest moon, larger than Mercury, with a thick
  atmosphere and lakes of liquid methane.
- **Enceladus** — an icy moon that erupts water-vapor geysers from a
  subsurface ocean.

## Exploration

The **Cassini–Huygens** mission (2004–2017) revolutionized our understanding of
Saturn, its rings, and moons, and landed the Huygens probe on Titan.

# Europa

Europa is the smallest of Jupiter's four Galilean moons and one of the most
promising places to search for extraterrestrial life.

## Quick Facts

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Parent planet | Jupiter |
| Diameter | 3,122 km |
| Average distance from Jupiter | 671,000 km |
| Orbital period | 3.55 days |
| Surface temperature | ~−160 °C to −220 °C |

## Overview

Europa has a smooth, icy surface crisscrossed by reddish-brown cracks. Beneath
its ice shell lies a global saltwater ocean — estimated to contain twice as much
water as all of Earth's oceans combined. Tidal heating from Jupiter keeps this
ocean liquid.

## Why It Matters

The combination of liquid water, chemical energy, and the right elements makes
Europa a key target in astrobiology. If life exists, it may thrive near
hydrothermal vents on the ocean floor, similar to extremophiles on Earth.

## Exploration

- **NASA's Europa Clipper** (launched 2024) will conduct dozens of close
  flybys to assess the moon's habitability.
- **ESA's JUICE** mission will also study Europa along with Ganymede and
  Callisto.

# Titan

Titan is Saturn's largest moon and the second largest in the Solar System —
the only moon with a substantial atmosphere.

## Quick Facts

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Parent planet | Saturn |
| Diameter | 5,150 km |
| Average distance from Saturn | 1.22 million km |
| Orbital period | 15.9 days |
| Surface temperature | ~−179 °C |

## Overview

Titan is larger than the planet Mercury and is the only known world besides
Earth with stable liquid on its surface. Its dense, nitrogen-rich atmosphere is
hazy with orange hydrocarbons. Instead of water, Titan has lakes and rivers of
liquid methane and ethane.

## Notable Features

- **Methane cycle** — Titan has a "hydrological" cycle of methane rain, rivers,
  lakes, and clouds, analogous to Earth's water cycle.
- **Kraken Mare** — the largest known liquid methane sea, comparable in size to
  the Caspian Sea.
- A possible subsurface water ocean beneath the icy crust.

## Exploration

The **Huygens probe** landed on Titan in 2005 — the most distant landing ever
achieved. NASA's **Dragonfly** rotorcraft mission, planned for the 2030s, will
fly across Titan's surface to study its chemistry and habitability.

# The Sun

The Sun is the star at the center of the Solar System and by far its most
massive object, containing 99.86% of the system's total mass.

## Quick Facts

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Type | G2V main-sequence star (yellow dwarf) |
| Diameter | 1,391,000 km (109 Earth) |
| Mass | 1.99 × 10³⁰ kg (333,000 Earth) |
| Surface temperature | ~5,500 °C |
| Core temperature | ~15 million °C |
| Age | ~4.6 billion years |
| Composition | ~73% hydrogen, ~25% helium |

## Overview

The Sun generates energy through nuclear fusion, converting about 600 million
tons of hydrogen into helium every second. This energy radiates outward as
light and heat, sustaining nearly all life on Earth. Light from the Sun takes
about 8 minutes and 20 seconds to reach Earth.

## Structure

- **Core** — where fusion occurs.
- **Radiative and convective zones** — transport energy outward.
- **Photosphere** — the visible surface.
- **Chromosphere and corona** — the outer atmosphere, visible during total
  solar eclipses.

## Solar Activity

The Sun follows an 11-year cycle of magnetic activity, producing sunspots,
solar flares, and coronal mass ejections that drive space weather and auroras.
In about 5 billion years, the Sun will expand into a red giant and eventually
become a white dwarf.

# Black Holes

A black hole is a region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing —
not even light — can escape from it.

## Overview

Black holes form when massive objects collapse under their own gravity. The
boundary beyond which nothing can return is called the **event horizon**. At the
center lies the **singularity**, a point of theoretically infinite density.

## Types of Black Holes

| Type | Mass range | Origin |
|------|------------|--------|
| Stellar-mass | ~3–100 solar masses | Collapse of a massive star |
| Intermediate-mass | 100–100,000 solar masses | Mergers / uncertain |
| Supermassive | Millions to billions of solar masses | Centers of galaxies |

## Key Concepts

- **Event horizon** — the point of no return.
- **Accretion disk** — superheated matter spiraling inward, emitting intense
  radiation.
- **Hawking radiation** — a theoretical process by which black holes slowly
  lose mass and energy over time.
- **Spaghettification** — the stretching of objects by extreme tidal forces
  near a black hole.

## Milestones

- **2015** — LIGO detected gravitational waves from two merging black holes.
- **2019** — The Event Horizon Telescope captured the first image of a black
  hole, in galaxy M87.
- **2022** — The first image of Sagittarius A*, the Milky Way's central black
  hole, was released.

# The Voyager Program

The Voyager program consists of two robotic probes, Voyager 1 and Voyager 2,
launched by NASA in 1977 to study the outer Solar System and beyond.

## Quick Facts

| Property | Voyager 1 | Voyager 2 |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| Launch date | Sep 5, 1977 | Aug 20, 1977 |
| Status | Active, in interstellar space | Active, in interstellar space |
| Entered interstellar space | 2012 | 2018 |

## Overview

The Voyagers took advantage of a rare planetary alignment that occurs once
every 175 years, allowing them to visit multiple outer planets using
gravitational slingshots. Voyager 2 remains the only spacecraft to have visited
all four giant planets: Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune.

## Major Discoveries

- Active volcanoes on Jupiter's moon Io.
- Detailed structure of Saturn's rings.
- The first close-up views of Uranus and Neptune.
- Hints of a subsurface ocean on Europa.

## The Golden Record

Each probe carries a gold-plated copper disk containing sounds and images
selected to portray the diversity of life and culture on Earth — a message for
any intelligent life that might find it. The Voyagers are now the most distant
human-made objects in existence.

# The Apollo Program

The Apollo program was NASA's effort to land humans on the Moon and bring them
safely back to Earth, carried out between 1961 and 1972.

## Overview

Initiated in response to President Kennedy's 1961 goal of landing a man on the
Moon before the end of the decade, Apollo became one of the greatest
achievements in human history. It used the massive **Saturn V** rocket, still
the most powerful rocket ever flown to operational service.

## Key Missions

| Mission | Year | Significance |
|---------|------|--------------|
| Apollo 1 | 1967 | Cabin fire killed three astronauts during a test |
| Apollo 8 | 1968 | First crewed mission to orbit the Moon |
| Apollo 11 | 1969 | First crewed Moon landing |
| Apollo 13 | 1970 | "Successful failure" — crew survived an explosion |
| Apollo 17 | 1972 | Last crewed mission to the Moon |

## Apollo 11

On July 20, 1969, Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin became the first humans to
walk on the Moon, while Michael Collins orbited above. Armstrong's words — "That's
one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind" — were broadcast to an
estimated 650 million people.

## Legacy

Twelve astronauts walked on the Moon across six successful landings, returning
382 kg of lunar samples that continue to inform our understanding of the Moon's
origin and the early Solar System.

# James Webb Space Telescope

The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) is the largest and most powerful space
telescope ever built, launched on December 25, 2021.

## Quick Facts

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Launch date | December 25, 2021 |
| Operator | NASA / ESA / CSA |
| Primary mirror | 6.5 m (18 gold-coated segments) |
| Orbit | Sun–Earth Lagrange point 2 (L2), ~1.5 million km from Earth |
| Primary wavelength | Infrared |

## Overview

Unlike the Hubble Space Telescope, which observes mainly in visible light, JWST
observes in infrared. This allows it to see through cosmic dust and to detect
the faint, redshifted light from the most distant and earliest galaxies in the
universe. A five-layer sunshield the size of a tennis court keeps its
instruments extremely cold (around −233 °C).

## Scientific Goals

- Observe the first galaxies that formed after the Big Bang.
- Study the formation of stars and planetary systems.
- Analyze the atmospheres of exoplanets for signs of habitability.
- Investigate objects within our own Solar System.

## Early Results

JWST has captured stunning images of nebulae, distant galaxies, and exoplanet
atmospheres, including the detection of carbon dioxide and other molecules in
worlds orbiting other stars — ushering in a new era of astronomy.

# The International Space Station

The International Space Station (ISS) is a large, habitable spacecraft in low
Earth orbit — a collaborative project among five space agencies.

## Quick Facts

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Partners | NASA, Roscosmos, ESA, JAXA, CSA |
| First module launched | 1998 (Zarya) |
| Continuously crewed since | November 2000 |
| Orbital altitude | ~400 km |
| Orbital speed | ~28,000 km/h |
| Time per orbit | ~90 minutes |
| Mass | ~420,000 kg |

## Overview

The ISS serves as a microgravity laboratory where crews conduct experiments in
biology, physics, astronomy, and materials science that are impossible on Earth.
It orbits the planet about 16 times per day, meaning astronauts see roughly 16
sunrises and sunsets daily.

## Life Aboard

- Crews typically stay for about six months.
- Everything from sleeping to eating must account for weightlessness.
- Water is recycled, including moisture from the air and urine.
- Regular exercise (about 2 hours/day) counteracts muscle and bone loss.

## Legacy and Future

Continuously occupied for over two decades, the ISS represents the longest
sustained human presence in space. It is expected to be retired around 2030,
with commercial space stations planned to succeed it.
